name: Paper and Ink Media
tagline: Thin cellulose sheets and fluid pigments that make writing lightweight, foldable, and mass-producible
problem:
Writing on stone or clay is durable but heavy; carrying a library on your back demands something thinner. Paper — interlocked plant fibres matted into sheets — trades archival perfection for portability and speed. That bargain creates new anxieties: mould in humid climates, acid that yellows pages, and fires that erase cities in an afternoon.

Ink is not decoration; it is colloidal chemistry. Pigments must disperse finely enough not to clog quills yet stay suspended; binders must fix to fibres without feathering uncontrollably. Watercolour skies and legal contracts ask different recipes from the same word ‘ink’.

Paper also democratised bureaucracy: forms, receipts, and textbooks scale when sheets become cheap. With scale came forgery, watermark arms races, and the quiet politics of who owns a mill upstream of a city.

For recovery, protect rag supply chains and sizing recipes as carefully as you protect forges — a papermaker is a chemist with a vat, not a woodcutter with a whim.
overview:
Traditional rag paper pulps linen and cotton cloth to long fibres, beats them in water, forms sheets on a wire mould, presses, dries, and often sizes with gelatin or starch so ink sits on the surface instead of blotting sideways. Wood pulp later lowered cost but introduced lignin that ages unless removed or buffered.

Carbon black inks soot in gum arabic are forgiving for scribes; iron-gall inks bite into fibres permanently but need disciplined chemistry — excess acid consumes paper from within. Printing inks add drying oils and modifiers for tack across metal type.

Watermarks and chain lines become security features and dating clues. Archival storage fights relative humidity swings that stress bindings and encourages alkaline buffering where budgets allow.

Paper’s fragility pushed redundancy: duplicate scriptoria, dispersed copies, and eventually digitisation — each layer inherits paper’s affordances while trying to escape fire.

principles.name: Hydrogen bonding network
principles.explanation: Cellulose fibres mat through hydrogen bonds when drained — strength scales with beating energy and fibre length.

principles.name: Sizing chemistry
principles.explanation: Fill pores partially so ink sits atop; oversizing repels ink — balance is empirical.

principles.name: Lignin yellowing
principles.explanation: Mechanical wood pulp retains lignin that oxidises — newsprint is ephemeral by chemistry.

principles.name: Capillary wicking
principles.explanation: Porosity and fibre orientation set anisotropic spread — grain direction matters for folding and printing.

components.name: Pulp slurry
components.madeFrom: Rag or wood chips after cook

rawMaterials.name: Cellulosic fibre
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Cotton and linen rag historically; softwood kraft pulp today; in closed habitats recycle textile trim and agricultural straw pulps with careful lignin control

rawMaterials.name: Water
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: River or RO-purified process water on Earth; closed-loop filtration and settling tanks where discharge is constrained

rawMaterials.name: Pigments
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Carbon black and organic dyes; mineral pigments like ochre where particle size is controlled — always pair pigment choice with intended pen or brush
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Disperse to submicron to avoid clogging

buildSteps.title: Source long clean fibres
buildSteps.description: Collect white linen or cotton rags free of buttons, elastic, and synthetic blends. Cut small and soak until fibres separate easily when rubbed. Note seasonal water hardness — minerals affect colour and sizing.

buildSteps.title: Beat to controlled fibrillation
buildSteps.description: Move from stamp mill or Hollander beater in timed passes; pull test sheets often. Stop when fibres knit on a wire but do not yet dust apart when dry — over-beating weakens sheets.

buildSteps.title: Sheet formation and couching
buildSteps.description: Dip a mould and deckle, shake to interlock fibres evenly, drain, couch onto felts, press gradually. Keep vat temperature steady — cold slows drainage; heat grows bacteria.
buildSteps.warningNote: Uneven shaking makes thin patches that tear

buildSteps.title: Dry under tension
buildSteps.description: Air-dry on lines or in loft spaces with gentle airflow; restrain edges if cockling appears. For writing-grade, apply size after partial drying per local recipe.

buildSteps.title: Mill ink and test on scraps
buildSteps.description: Disperse pigment with muller or professional disperser; record binder ratios. Test feathering, drying time, and bleed-through on your actual paper — not on modern coated stock.
buildSteps.warningNote: Iron-gall needs dated labels — it evolves acidity over decades

history.event: Cai Lun associated with papermaking improvement in Chinese tradition.
Paper mill established in Xàtiva region — spreads in Islamic world and Europe.

inventors.name: Cai Lun
inventors.contribution: Traditional attribution for Han-era papermaking improvements

impact: Paper lowered the mass-per-word of bureaucracy and scholarship — prerequisite for printing’s economics and for modern notebooks that think alongside engineers.

videos.title: Hand papermaking
videos.description: Craft demonstrations of mould and deckle technique.

sources.title: Library of Congress — Preservation Directorate
sources.note: Paper conservation, inks, and stabilisation for library collections
